source_citation: Polybius VI; Roman constitution-makers explicitly studied Greek models (Mouritsen 2017 Politics in the Roman Republic)
Herodotus Histories; Greek democratic identity constituted against Persian despotism (Hall 1989 Inventing the Barbarian)
Arrian Anabasis; Chandragupta modelled administration on Achaemenid precedents after Alexander (Thapar 1973 Asoka and the Decline of the Mauryas)
Venetian constitution self-consciously modelled on Roman republican mixed constitution (Lane 1973 Venice; Queller 1986 The Venetian Patriciate)
Han dynasty institutionalised Zhou ritual-political framework via Confucian synthesis (Loewe 1986 Cambridge History of China vol 1)
Tang restored Confucian bureaucratic examination system; direct institutional continuity (Twitchett 1979 Cambridge History of China vol 3)
Song civil service examinations and administrative law directly developed Tang precedents (Chaffee 1985 The Thorny Gates of Learning)
Ming founder explicitly invoked Song civilian governance models in founding legislation (Farmer 1995 Zhu Yuanzhang and Early Ming Legislation)
Tokugawa adopted Neo-Confucian political philosophy via Zhu Xi school from Ming China (Ooms 1985 Tokugawa Ideology)
Meiji Restoration explicitly defined against Tokugawa system; direct institutional overthrow (Beasley 1972 The Meiji Restoration)
Ottoman imperial ideology and administrative law drew on Abbasid caliphal precedents (Inalcik 1973 The Ottoman Empire; Imber 2002)
Habsburg emperors claimed Carolingian succession through translatio imperii (Wilson 2016 Heart of Europe; Bryce 1864)
Contemporaneous non-territorial high-freedom polities; mutual awareness documented (Brady 1985 Turning Swiss)
Weimar constitutional assembly studied British parliamentary model (Huber 1978 Deutsche Verfassungsgeschichte; Nicholls 1979)
Nazi regime explicitly defined against Weimar; direct institutional replacement (Evans 2003 The Coming of the Third Reich)
German liberals studied French Third Republic as democratic model (Winkler 2006 Germany: The Long Road West)
American founders explicitly engaged with and modified British parliamentary tradition (Wood 1969 The Creation of the American Republic; Bailyn 1967)
Weimar and Soviet systems explicitly opposed; mutual ideological definition (Carr 1950 The Bolshevik Revolution; Mommsen 1996)
Nazi and Soviet systems explicitly constituted as antipodal; mutual ideological opposition (Snyder 2010 Bloodlands; Evans 2005)
CCP party-state organisation and ideology directly modelled on Soviet system (Meisner 1999 Mao's China and After)
Norwegian social-democratic model developed as explicit alternative to US liberal capitalism (Esping-Andersen 1990 Three Worlds of Welfare Capitalism)
Venetian republican thought cited in Swiss political writing; shared non-territorial model (Pocock 1975 The Machiavellian Moment)
Lee Kuan Yew explicitly adapted and departed from Westminster model (Lee 2000 From Third World to First; Josey 1968)
Cold War mutual ideological opposition; each defined foreign policy against the other (Gaddis 1997 We Now Know)
Both cited as assembly-based polities in medieval European political thought; indirect parallel
Hanseatic merchants active in Iceland; documented trading contact (Johannesson 1974 A History of the Old Icelandic Commonwealth)
Jesuit reports explicitly compared Mughal and Chinese imperial systems (Ricci 1615 Della entrata della Compagnia di Giesù; Findley 2005)
Contemporaneous Islamic empires with documented diplomatic contact and shared caliphal claims (Fleischer 1986 Bureaucrat and Intellectual in the Ottoman Empire)
Spanish (Habsburg) conquest directly replaced Aztec governance; colonial administration documented (Gibson 1964 The Aztecs Under Spanish Rule)
Carolingian coronation 800 CE explicitly invoked Roman imperial model (McKitterick 2008 Charlemagne: The Formation of a European Identity)
Renaissance humanists invoked Athenian precedents in Swiss cantonal political thought (Hankins 2019 Virtue Politics)
French republicans studied Westminster model during Third Republic constitution (Rosanvallon 2007 The Demands of Liberty)
Hugo Preuss studied US federalism in drafting Weimar constitution (Caldwell 1997 Popular Sovereignty and the Crisis of German Constitutional Law)
Roman law and republican theory central to English constitutional thought; Harrington; Cicero in Founders (Pettit 1997 Republicanism)
Harrington Oceana (1656) used Venice as primary model for English republicanism (Pocock 1975 The Machiavellian Moment)
Founders cited Athens extensively; Federalist Papers discuss Athenian democracy (Ober 2008 Democracy and Knowledge)
French republicans invoked Athenian precedent; Fustel de Coulanges; Third Republic classicism (Hartog 2005 Ancients Moderns Savages)
French republican tradition explicitly Roman: Marianne; fasces; senatorial imagery (Furet 1988 The French Revolution)
Mughal rulers consciously positioned within Ashokan imperial tradition; Akbar invoked Ashoka (Richards 1993 The Mughal Empire)
Ottoman-Habsburg military and ideological opposition 1529-1683; each defined imperial identity against the other (Ágoston 2021 The Last Muslim Conquest)
British encounter with Qing bureaucracy; Orientalist contrast between Chinese despotism and British liberty (Viner 1953; Bayly 2004 The Birth of the Modern World)
Macartney Mission 1793; British Parliamentary reports explicitly contrasted Ming/Qing governance with British constitution (Cranmer-Byng 1962 An Embassy to China)
Carolingian-Abbasid diplomatic contact documented; Charlemagne embassy to Harun al-Rashid 797 CE (McKitterick 2008)
Post-1945 Cold War ideological opposition; each defined foreign policy against the other (Gaddis 2005 The Cold War)
Norwegian constitution (1814) modelled on British parliamentary system; Westminster-derived (Sejersted 2011 Freedom and Equality)
Singapore and PRC explicitly contrasted developmental models; Lee-Deng ideological exchange documented (Vogel 2011 Deng Xiaoping and the Transformation of China)
Meiji constitution-makers studied British; Prussian; French models; Ito Hirobumi studied Westminster (Pittau 1967 Political Thought in Early Meiji Japan)
Meiji Japan studied US constitutional model alongside British; Mori Arinori in Washington (Pittau 1967)
Tang dynasty Confucian scholars explicitly studied Zhou rites as normative template; Zhou li central to Tang administrative code (McMullen 1988 State and Scholars in Tang China)
US defined war aims explicitly against Nazi governance; Roosevelt Four Freedoms 1941; Atlantic Charter; post-war US occupation directly replaced Nazi institutions (Mazower 2008 Hitlers Empire)
Churchill explicitly framed WWII as democracy vs tyranny; Nazi ideology defined against British parliamentary liberalism (Evans 2008 The Third Reich at War)
Tanzimat reformers (1839-1876) explicitly studied British parliamentary and constitutional models; Mustafa Reshid Pasha in London (Shaw & Shaw 1977 History of the Ottoman Empire and Modern Turkey)

East India Company-Mughal contact from 1608; British parliamentary debates on India governance explicitly contrasted British and Mughal systems (Bayly 1988 Indian Society and the Making of the British Empire)

CCP explicitly rejected Confucianism (May Fourth Movement legacy) then partially rehabilitated it post-1978; explicit ideological positioning (Goldman & Lee 2002 An Intellectual History of Modern China)

Meiji Restoration explicitly rejected Confucian civil service model; Imperial Rescript on Education negotiated between Confucian and Western frameworks (Pittau 1967 Political Thought in Early Meiji Japan; Gluck 1985 Japans Modern Myths)
Norwegian political culture draws on shared Norse assembly tradition (thing); Icelandic Althing cited in Scandinavian democratic historiography (Sejersted 2011 Freedom and Equality)
